title: Data sources
text:
It is a data catalog containing environmental monitoring data, in consistent, analysis-ready formats accessible from Azure Blob Storage: https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/catalog
To access data, there is a need to be registered. In order to automatize the process of login, a «sign_url» function has been developed and added into the Nostradamus customized cube in a box

title: Definitions
text:
Open data cube (ODC)
“free, open-source software package that simplifies the management and analysis of large amounts of satellite imagery and other Earth observation data”
“allows users to easily access, process, and analyze decades of geographical data to track changes on Earth’s surface over time”
https://www.opendatacube.org

title: Nostradamus Frequently Asked Questions
text: I want to clone the the Nostradamus jupyter cube into my local Docker application (git clone https://git.unepgrid.ch/NOSTRADAMUS/cube-in-a-box-jupyter.git) but it says « fatal: destination path ‘cube-in-a-box-jupyter’ already exists and is not an empty directory »

title: Landsat
text:
Landsat is an Earth observation satellite programme that operates dedicated satellites continuously since 1972 through 9 Landsat programmes.



Credit: NASA’s Scientific Visualization Studio


The Landsat archive of raw and processed data since 1972 is organized into 3 collections:

Pre-collection (before 2016): old on-demand processing system, no longer available
Collection 1 (2016-2022): tiered collection management structure, ensuring that all Landsat Level-1 products provide a consistent archive of known data quality to support time-series analyses and data “stacking”. No longer available
Collection 2 (2022-present): reprocessing of the Collection 1 archive resulting in several product improvements that harness recent advancements in data processing, algorithm development, and data access and distribution capabilities: alignment of the pixels, sensors value coherence, metadata quality, etc to have a more accurate product

Each collection organises data into levels of processing:

Level1 (L1): raw data corrected for geometry and topography
Level2 (L2): L1 images are taken as the basis, on which physical values of the atmosphere (aerosols, ozone, water vapor, …) are corrected => ready data for certain analyses (indices calculation, multi-temporal series, etc.) that could not be done with L1 images
Level3 (L3): analytical derived products from L2, that are automatically generated

Among the various products available in the Nostradamus datacube, the one called “lsX_c2l2_sp.odc-product.yaml“ is the Landsat one, made of USGS Landsat 8 and 9 Collection 2 Level-2 Science Products, consisting of atmospherically corrected surface reflectance and surface temperature image data.

title: Landsat
text:
Landsat is an Earth observation satellite programme that operates dedicated satellites continuously since 1972 through 9 Landsat programmes.



Credit: NASA’s Scientific Visualization Studio


The Landsat archive of raw and processed data since 1972 is organized into 3 collections:

Pre-collection (before 2016): old on-demand processing system, no longer available
Collection 1 (2016-2022): tiered collection management structure, ensuring that all Landsat Level-1 products provide a consistent archive of known data quality to support time-series analyses and data “stacking”. No longer available
Collection 2 (2022-present): reprocessing of the Collection 1 archive resulting in several product improvements that harness recent advancements in data processing, algorithm development, and data access and distribution capabilities: alignment of the pixels, sensors value coherence, metadata quality, etc to have a more accurate product

Each collection organises data into levels of processing:

Level1 (L1): raw data corrected for geometry and topography
Level2 (L2): L1 images are taken as the basis, on which physical values of the atmosphere (aerosols, ozone, water vapor, …) are corrected => ready data for certain analyses (indices calculation, multi-temporal series, etc.) that could not be done with L1 images
Level3 (L3): analytical derived products from L2, that are automatically generated

Among the various products available in the Nostradamus datacube, the one called “lsX_c2l2_sp.odc-product.yaml“ is the Landsat one, made of USGS Landsat 8 and 9 Collection 2 Level-2 Science Products, consisting of atmospherically corrected surface reflectance and surface temperature image data.

title: Nostradamus datacube
text:
A dedicated cube in a box has been deployed for the Nostradamus project. It is a modifed clone from the original cube in a box.
The following modifications have been done on the original cube in a box to become the Nostradamus data cube:

Default Jupyter notebook replaced by Jupyterlab
“sign_url” function added to access data in «Planetary Computer»
Default source for Sentinel-2 has been replaced by Planetary Computer as it was slow and unstable
The default ESRI Land Cover source in Planetary Computer (https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/dataset/io-lulc) that is deprecated has been replaced by https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/dataset/io-lulc-annual-v02
Landsat Collection 2 Level 2 Science Products added (see slide
Jupyter notebook modified or created for each available Level2 science product (see slides 6 to 8)
Datacube-explorer added and modified to access Planetary Computer data
DATETIME added as make argument (allows to filter the time range of interest)

title: Jupyter
text:
Jupyter is an open-source project that supports interactive data science and scientific computing across all programming languages. The Jupyter ecosystem is made of several subprojects that propose many different software.
Among them, Jupyter notebook, JupyterLab and JupyterHub are relevant for the use of the Nostradamus datacube.

title: Jupyter
text:
Jupyter notebook
A Jupyter notebook is an interactive document with that is run in your web broswer and allows to have in a single .ipynb file:

executable code (Python, R, …) that can be run cell by cell
formatted text in MarkDown language
visualisations (graphs, maps, tables) that interactively display below the code



How to install it on my machine?
Type the following command in your Terminal:
pip install notebook
Note: in some cases you might need to write
pip3 install notebook


How to know if it is installed on my machine?
Type the following command in your Terminal:
jupyter --version
If it is installed, you should see its version
If it is not installed, you will see a message like “command not found”.


How to launch it?
Type the following command in your Terminal:
jupyter notebook
This should open you navigator at http://localhost:8888.


How to use it?
Once you navigator is open at http://localhost:8888, use the top menus to either create new .ipynb file or open an existing one.

title: Jupyter
text:
Jupyterlab

JupyterLab is the modern version of Jupyter notebooks. It is a full environment that contains more features than Jupyter notebooks. Here are some additional elements compared to Jupyter notebooks:

multi-tabs
Integrated file explorer
Text and code editor
Integrated terminal
Extensions


How to install it on my machine?
There are 2 main options to have Jupyterlab on your machine: (A) either as a desktop application or (B) using your web browser
(A) Installation of Jupyterlab Desktop
Go to https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-desktop and download the application corresponding to your operating system






Caution



Since August 2025, Jupyter desktop is not actively maintained and does not receive security bug fixes


(B) Installation of Jupyterlab
Type the following command in your Terminal:
pip install jupyterlab
Note: in some cases you might need to write
pip3 install jupyterlab


How to know if it is installed on my machine?
Type the following command in your Terminal:
jupyter lab --version
If it is installed, you should see its version


How to launch it?
Type the following command in your Terminal:
jupyter lab
This should open the Jupyterlab environment in your broswer at http://localhost:8888/lab



How to use it?
Once you navigator is open at http://localhost:8888/lab, use the various menus to navigate through your files and folders and to create new ones.

title: Jupyter
text:
JupyterHub

JupyterHub is a multi-users server, allowing each member of a team to access his/her own JupyterLab environment

How to access a JupyterHub instance?
There should normally be 2 options:

Either your sysadmin has installed JupyterHub on a server and you can access it through a url and login
or a self-hosted JupyterHub Docker image has been created and each user can use it as an isolated environment

This Docker solution is what has been chosen for the Nostradamus JupyterHub.

title: UNIGE & UNEP/GRID-Geneva data cubes Capacity Building
text:
This website contains capacity building material related to data cubes developed by UNIGE and UNEP/GRID-Geneva for various projects.
It aims at improving the understanding of data cube concepts and their use in the projects mentioned, through detailed explanations and dedicated exercices.

title: Data cubes

title: Nostradamus datacube setup
text:
Install Docker desktop for your operating system from https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop and launch it
To make sure all works fine, type the following commands in your Terminal (example on Mac OS):

docker –-version docker compose version
=> if corresponding information is returned, it mean alls is working fine for your Docker session and you can proceed to the next steps

Clone the Nostradamus jupyter cube into your local Docker application by typing the following command: git clone https://git.unepgrid.ch/NOSTRADAMUS/cube-in-a-box-jupyter.git

=> This will create a cube-in-a-box-jupyter folder with all the necessary Docker files (in case the folder already exists, use the pull command, see in the FAQ at the end of the presentation)

Once the installation is finalized, go to the newly created folder by typing in your Terminal cd cube-in-a-box-jupyter
Use then the make setup command to initiate the datacube before using it. You can either simply type make setup , which will deploy the datacube with all layers available, or you can filter a specific area of interest using the BBOX parameter (e.g. make setup BBOX=5.95,45.81,10.50,47.81) and the DATETIME parameter specifying a particular time range (e.g. make setup BBOX=5.95,45.81,10.50,47.81 DATETIME=2024-01-01/2024-12-31)

title: Planetary computer
text:
It is a data catalog containing environmental monitoring data, in consistent, analysis-ready formats accessible from Azure Blob Storage. Access it from here .
Data layers are categorized into 18 categories to facilitiate their discoverability.
To access planetary computer data, there is a need to be registered. In order to automatize the process of login, a «sign_url» function has been developed and added into the Nostradamus customized cube in a box.

title: Welcome to this Jupyter Notebook Demo
text:
This is a simple demonstration notebook to show you the basics of how Jupyter Notebooks work.
You will find Markdown cells (like this one) for text and explanations, and Code cells (like the one below) where you can write and execute Python code.
Click on the code cell below and press Shift + Enter to run it.

from datetime import datetime

# Ask the user for their first name
first_name = input("Please enter your first name: ")

# Get the current date and time
now = datetime.now()
formatted_date = now.strftime("%A, %B %d, %Y at %H:%M")

# Greet the user
print(f"\nWelcome, {first_name}!")
print(f"Today is {formatted_date}.")
print("\nHave a wonderful day! 😊")

Please enter your first name:  Yaniss



Welcome, Yaniss!
Today is Friday, March 13, 2026 at 16:24.

Have a wonderful day! 😊
